title: The Traitor's Gambit
summary: The heroes are drawn into a cantina confrontation on Jakku, discover a stolen Imperial code cylinder, use it to download the coordinates of a secret prison, and break Admiral Varth out of a jungle fortress — all for the promise of a stolen fortune.

marks.label: Strange Bedfellows
marks.desc: A stranger crashes your table bleeding. You chose to fight for her instead of walking away.

marks.label: The Devil You Know
marks.desc: Strike a deal with Switch — a droid who trades in secrets. What you owe him may cost more than credits.

marks.label: Ghost in the Machine
marks.desc: Use the Code Cylinder to access the Imperial shuttle's nav computer and learn where it points.

marks.label: Into the Green Hell
marks.desc: Survive the jungle of Ajan Kloss — the terrain, the wildlife, and the things that live between the trees.

marks.label: The Warden's Price
marks.desc: Mandrake offers a deal: his guidance for your muscle. Honor it or betray it — either way, there's a cost.

marks.label: The Vanishing Place
marks.desc: Breach the fortress and reach the man the Empire buried.

marks.label: The Extraction
marks.desc: Get Varth out alive. Whatever it takes.

marks.label: The Crucible
marks.desc: The plan catastrophically fails. Improvise under fire to survive.

gearNotes.reason: Toxic fungal spores in the Ajan Kloss jungle. Without respiratory protection, characters take environmental penalties on all physical actions during the trek.
Jungle traversal — crossing gaps, climbing, rappelling. Without rope, certain routes become impossible or require much harder Athletics checks. The item everyone forgets to buy.
The Vanishing Place fortress has electronic locks, laser fences, and security panels. Without one, bypassing these is stepped down or impossible.
Generator sabotage as part of Mandrake's deal. Without proper tools, Tech actions are stepped down.
The Banshee is damaged on landing. Without a starship tool kit, all repair rolls are stepped down. Hard permission gate for getting off-world.
Multiple combats across both parts with no medical facilities. At least one is non-negotiable.
The Reestkii Landing Field read-aloud literally references macrobinoculars for recon. Also useful for jungle scouting.
Acid pools in the jungle are disguised as standing water. A tox detector identifies them without someone stepping in one.
Dim cantina, underground bunker, dense jungle canopy — multiple dark environments across both parts.
The bluff approach at the Reestkii hangar requires impersonating Imperial crew or authorized personnel.
Covering retreat across open ground during the three-way climax with Shadow Troopers flanking.
Mid-combat trauma clearance. Valuable in the multi-fight climax sequence.
Extended jungle trek. Fatigue penalties stack after 48 hours without food.
Jakku desert heat in Part 1. Environmental comfort only — no hard gate.
Coordinating with Maya for extraction timing. Encrypted channel prevents Imperial interception.
Hacking terminals in the fortress Comms Center to intercept transmissions about Draco's arrival.

scenes.title: Bad Business at The Burning Deck
scenes.subtitle: In Media Res
scenes.readAloudPart1:
Jakku. A world the galaxy forgot... if it ever noticed. Out here on the Western Reaches, the holonet feeds are weeks old, the Republic is a memory, and the Empire is a rumor that sometimes wears a uniform. You've heard the stories filtering through the spacer lanes: whole systems pacified, Separatist holdouts burned out of their bunkers, the clone army quietly replaced by men who volunteer. The Core worlds call it order. Out here, people just call it the new way things are.

Reestkii squats on the desert flats like something the sand is trying to swallow. Drifters. Smugglers. Moisture farmers with nothing left to farm. Varga the Hutt runs what passes for an economy: debt, protection, and the quiet understanding that Imperial patrols from Arkanis Sector come through maybe twice a standard year... and they never stay.

You stepped off a rust-bucket passenger transport twelve hours ago. All of you. However you found each other, you're a crew now, or close enough to one. You eat together. You watch each other's backs. You argue about whose turn it is to pay for fuel. And right now you're waiting together, which might be the hardest part. A fixer named Cade Ryll put the word out through the spacer lanes: crew needed for a short-haul job, good pay, no questions. He named this cantina. He named a time. That was an hour ago.

The Burning Deck. That's what passes for a watering hole at the edge of civilization. The name's painted over whatever it was called before, which was painted over the name before that. Low ceiling. Failing glow-rods. The air thick with the smell of engine coolant and cheap Corellian whiskey. A Devaronian sits alone at the sabacc table in the far alcove, turning a credit chip across his knuckles. Watching everything. Playing nothing. A pair of Abyssin drifters argue in the corner booth over a rusted motivator, their guttural voices carrying even through the cantina noise. A weathered Togruta woman nurses a drink at the bar, her montrals scarred in a way that says ex-military. She hasn't looked up once, but her hand never strays far from her hip. Behind the bar, a nervous Bith wipes the same glass he's been wiping for twenty minutes, his oversized eyes flicking to the door every time the wind rattles the blast shutters.

The Burning Deck is quieter than it should be tonight. You noticed it when you docked: a Lambda-class shuttle, sitting on the landing field, flanked by two stormtroopers standing in the heat like they were carved from plastoid. Nobody docks a Lambda on Jakku without a reason. Fewer faces in the stalls on the walk in. The regulars are nursing their drinks a little harder. Talking a little softer. Something has changed the arithmetic, and nobody knows the new numbers yet.

Your drinks are warm. Your fixer is an hour late. And nothing on Jakku is going the way it was supposed to.
scenes.readAloudPart1PauseNote: PAUSE HERE. Let the players absorb the cantina. Ask: 'What do you do while you wait?' Give them time to look around, talk to each other, interact with the patrons, ask the bartender about the Imperial shuttle, or just stew. When they've had their moment — or when the energy needs a push — move to Part 2.
scenes.readAloudPart2:
The blast doors hiss open, and the heat pours in.

A young human woman. Dark hair, flight jacket, maybe twenty-five. She half-runs, half-stumbles through the entrance. One hand is pressed against her ribs, where her jacket is scorched through. She's bleeding. The Bith bartender freezes mid-wipe. The Devaronian at the sabacc table looks up for the first time all night.

She makes it four steps before her boot catches a chair leg and she pitches forward, straight into your table. Drinks scatter. Her hands hit the surface and she catches herself, breathing hard, leaving a smear of blood on the table edge. For a second she just stares at you, wild-eyed, like she's trying to decide if you're a wall, or a door.

"Sorry," she manages between breaths. Then, quieter: "Don't move."

Before anyone can ask what that means, three figures fill the entrance. A heavy-set Weequay with a scarred knuckle-duster on his right hand, and two Kyuzo in wide-brimmed war helms. Their blasters are holstered. They're not here to kill anyone. They're here to take something back. The Weequay rolls his shoulders, looks at the woman braced against your table, looks at all of you sitting around it, and makes a calculation.

"The girl took something that belongs to Varga the Hutt," he says. "This doesn't have to involve anyone else."

scenes.gmNotes:
TWO-BEAT STRUCTURE: This scene is split into two read-aloud blocks. Read Part 1 (the cantina, the crew waiting, the patrons, the atmosphere) and then PAUSE. Ask 'What do you do while you wait?' Let the players explore the room, talk to each other, interact with patrons, investigate the Imperial shuttle — whatever they want. This is their first moment in the world. Don't rush it. When the energy is right — or when they need a push — read Part 2 (Maya's entrance).

  CADE RYLL: The fixer who brought them here never shows. He's a narrative device — he gave the crew a reason to be on Jakku, at this cantina, together. Whether he got cold feet, got arrested, or got killed is up to you. If the players ask around, nobody at The Burning Deck knows the name. This is intentional — it makes them feel stranded and primes them to listen when Maya offers an alternative.

  THE PATRONS: The cantina regulars are set dressing with depth if the players engage. The Devaronian (sabacc alcove) is a small-time gambler named Siv Keel — he knows the cantina well and can tell the PCs that the Burning Deck has been tense since the Lambda landed two days ago. The Abyssin drifters are arguing over a water claim in the badlands — they're loud but harmless and can point the PCs toward the deep desert if asked. The Togruta at the bar is Nela Bren — an ex-Republic military scout who deserted after Order 66. She won't volunteer much but responds to respect; she can confirm Imperial patrols have increased in the sector. The Bith bartender, Dodd Mull, is terrified — he owes Varga money and is afraid any trouble in his establishment will bring the Hutt's people down on him. All patrons flee toward B1/C1 (entrance) or A3 (back exit) if weapons come out.

  MAYA'S BACKSTORY (GM ONLY): Maya intercepted an Imperial Code Cylinder before Varga's operators could get their hands on it. She knew it was valuable — encrypted Imperial data, potentially worth a fortune to the right buyer. She thought she could deal with Varga: cut him in, negotiate a split, walk away clean. But Varga doesn't negotiate over things he already considers his. The cylinder was moving through his supply chain. In his eyes, she stole from him. He sent Krev's crew to recover it. They caught up to her outside the cantina — Krev put a warning shot into her ribs to slow her down. She ran anyway. The foot chase ended at The Burning Deck's blast doors.

  MAYA'S ENTRANCE: This is NOT an assassination scene. The enforcers are a retrieval crew for Varga's operation — they're here to recover the cylinder, not kill Maya. They have NOT drawn weapons inside the cantina. Maya's collision with the heroes' table looks like an accident — she tripped on a chair leg while running wounded. But the GM should know: she was already calculating. She clocked the armed crew at the table on her way in and angled toward them. The trip was real — she's hurt and off-balance — but the direction she ran was deliberate. The players should NOT know this yet. The Insight check (sc-s1-read-maya) is the gate for that information. To the players, she's just a wounded stranger who crashed into their table at the worst possible time.

  MAYA'S DIALOGUE: 'Sorry' and 'Don't move' are deliberately ambiguous. She's not explicitly asking them to play along or be her shield. She's just a hurt person trying to survive the next thirty seconds. The enforcers see her at the crew's table and make the assumption themselves — 'This doesn't have to involve anyone else' implies THEY think there might be a connection. The heroes can choose to confirm or deny that assumption.

  NARRATIVE PACING: This scene runs on narrative pacing. Do NOT call for dice unless failure forks the story. Maya crashing in — narrative. Krev's ultimatum — narrative. The bartender's nervousness — narrative. Only roll when a PC takes an action where failure has real consequences (escalation, loss of initiative, or missed intelligence).

  THE IMPERIAL SHUTTLE: Foreshadowing only. The Lambda on the landing field is not part of this scene — it seeds future tension. If players investigate during the pause, the stormtroopers say nothing. Locals don't know why it's here. Dodd the bartender nervously changes the subject. This pays off later.

  MAYA'S WOUND: Real. A blaster graze to her side — Krev's warning shot outside the cantina to slow her down during the foot chase. Non-lethal but painful and bleeding. She is desperate — genuinely — but she's also smarter than she looks.

  RESOLUTION PATHS:
  • QUICK-DRAW INTIMIDATION: A Gunslinger draws fast — blaster out before the enforcers can react. This IS the Intimidate check (Moderate), not a separate action. The draw is the statement. The blaster is leverage, not escalation. Success: Krev folds ('Not worth it today'). Failure: Krev calls the bluff ('You won't shoot over some girl you don't know') — now the PC must decide: pull the trigger (firefight) or holster (Krev smells weakness, uses Lean In).
  • SOCIAL: Charm, Deception, or Persuasion (Moderate) can convince the enforcers Maya isn't worth the trouble, redirect them, or negotiate a delay.
  • BRAWL: If anyone throws a punch, the Kyuzo respond in kind. Bar brawl — fists, headbutts, bottles. Stun damage. Blasters stay holstered.
  • FIREFIGHT: Only triggers if (a) a brawl is already happening AND someone draws a blaster, OR (b) the social check fails AND a PC with blaster already drawn refuses to hand Maya over. A drawn blaster alone is not an escalation — it's a negotiating tool. This is Star Wars.

  IF COMBAT TRIGGERS — [Join Battle] Reference:
  • Control Step-Down: Highest enemy Tier = 1 → step down Control Die 1 time
  • Failed Control (1-3): PC is surprised — [Disoriented] + [Exposed] until end of first turn
  • Mastery (8+): PC designates one enemy as surprised — [Disoriented] + [Exposed] on that NPC
  • Power Die result = initiative slot (higher acts first). NPCs act at static Initiative (Wits + Tier)
  • NPC static initiative: Krev = 2, Kyuzo = 1

  NPC Tactics:
  • Krev uses Loom (maneuver) to advance and Threaten (action) for social pressure. If brawl, he switches to Physique attacks.
  • Kyuzo use Reposition (maneuver, no reactions provoked) to flank — one to A2, one to D2. Then Hit and Run (action) for war-helm headbutts.

  KEY LORE: 16 BBY. Imperial presence on Jakku is thin — Arkanis Sector patrols, no garrison. The Lambda shuttle today is unusual and unsettling. Varga the Hutt runs the real economy here: smuggling, debt, protection. A cantina fight draws local attention (Varga's people, scavenger gangs) but NOT Imperial attention — the troopers at the landing field don't care about Hutt business.

scenes.hazards: Cantina environment — dim lighting, overturned tables, broken glass, panicking patrons. A fight on Jakku draws local attention (Varga's people, local gangs) but NOT Imperial attention.

decisions.choice: Help Maya against the enforcers
decisions.consequence: The heroes are now entangled in a heist. Maya offers the Code Cylinder job.

decisions.choice: Try to stay out of it
decisions.consequence: The enforcers assume the heroes are Maya's crew regardless — confrontation is unavoidable.

narrativeLinks.note: Varga's enforcers here foreshadow the full encounter with his organization on Takodana.

encounters.name: The Recovery
encounters.trigger: Immediate — thugs in the doorway
encounters.description: Varga's retrieval crew confronts Maya at the heroes' table. They want the Code Cylinder back — not blood. Heroes must decide whether to get involved. This is narrative pacing — no dice until a PC takes an action where failure matters.
encounters.tactics: Krev uses Loom (maneuver) — walks to the table, fills the space. Then Threaten (action) — social pressure via implied violence. Kyuzo silently Reposition to flank (one toward A2 booths, one toward D2 alcove). If a hero draws a blaster, that's leverage — it feeds into the Intimidate check, not a trigger for combat. Krev reassesses: is this stolen property worth a blaster bolt? If the Intimidate succeeds, he folds. If it fails, he calls the bluff and uses Lean In.

encounters.name: Cantina Brawl
encounters.trigger: Only if someone throws the first punch, or social resolution fails badly and PCs refuse to back down
encounters.description: Bar brawl in a cramped cantina. Fists, headbutts, thrown bottles, overturned tables. Stun damage — no one is trying to kill. Blasters stay holstered. Combat only escalates to lethal if: (a) a brawl is already happening AND someone draws a blaster, OR (b) social failed AND a PC with blaster drawn refuses to hand Maya over. [map:burning-deck]
encounters.tactics: Krev grapples and shoves (Physique 3). Kyuzo headbutt with war helms — Hit and Run (melee attack + free disengage), then Reposition to flank. They fight as a pair. If a blaster comes out during the brawl, all three draw — the scene shifts to lethal.

composition.positioning: Krev at B1/C1 entrance, Kyuzo split B1 and C1. Kyuzo Reposition to A2 and D2 to flank.

environmentMechanics.name: Dim Cantina Lighting
environmentMechanics.effect: Failing glow-rods create shadow pools. Ranged attacks at distance stepped down.
environmentMechanics.mitigation: Close range unaffected. Rodian infrared / Sullustan darkvision ignore penalty.

environmentMechanics.name: Cantina Furniture
environmentMechanics.effect: Overturned tables = light cover. Bar = hard cover. Chairs and bottles as improvised weapons.
environmentMechanics.mitigation: Athletics check to flip a table for instant cover.

environmentMechanics.name: Panicking Patrons
environmentMechanics.effect: Civilians scramble for exits. Ranged attacks into crowds risk bystanders.
environmentMechanics.mitigation: Careful shot placement or waiting for clear lines.

zones.label: Solid Wall
zones.terrain: Duracrete wall. Impassable. Channels all traffic through B1/C1 entrance.

zones.label: Entrance (West)
zones.terrain: Blast door entrance. Jakku sunlight pours in. No cover — anyone standing here is fully exposed. Door controls on the wall.

zones.label: Entrance (East)
zones.terrain: Blast door entrance. Wide enough for three abreast. No cover. The glare from outside imposes no penalty but is narratively blinding.

zones.label: Solid Wall
zones.terrain: Duracrete wall. Impassable. Storage crates stacked against the outside.

zones.label: Booth Row
zones.terrain: Curved booth seating along the wall. Light cover from booth backs. Shadow pool — dim lighting penalty applies to ranged attacks targeting or from this zone at range. A few patrons nursing drinks.

zones.label: Center Floor (PCs' Table)
zones.terrain: The heroes' table. Light cover from furniture. A PC can use Find Cover (maneuver) to flip the table for improved light cover. Central position — adjacent to all Row 2 and Row 1/3 zones. This is where the action starts.

zones.label: Open Floor
zones.terrain: Open cantina floor. No cover, no obstacles. Clear sightlines to every other zone. The kill zone if blasters come out.

zones.label: Alcove
zones.terrain: Recessed alcove with a sabacc table. Light cover from the table edge. Deep shadow — dim lighting penalty applies. A patron or two hiding here.

zones.label: Back Hallway
zones.terrain: Narrow corridor to the back exit. No cover. Leads to the alley behind the cantina — the escape route. Dim lighting from a single failing glow-rod.

zones.label: Bar (West)
zones.terrain: West end of the bar counter. Hard cover — the bar is solid durasteel and wood. Bottles and glasses as improvised weapons (Light chassis, stun). The bartender's position. Maya flees here if combat starts.

zones.label: Bar (East)
zones.terrain: East end of the bar counter. Hard cover. Tap system and storage underneath. Same improvised weapon access as B3. Good defensive position with sightlines to the entrance.

zones.label: Store Room
zones.terrain: Closed door (requires maneuver to open). Full cover once inside. Dark — no light source. Crates of supplies, kegs. Dead end — defensible but no exit. A cornered position.

startingPositions.notes: Seated at their table. Central position with light cover.
Crashes into B2. Flees to B3 (bar, hard cover) if combat starts.
Enters through blast doors. Exposed — no cover. Advances to B2/C2 via Loom maneuver.
Behind Krev at entrance. Repositions to A2 (booth row) to flank.
Flanking the entrance. Repositions to D2 (alcove) to cut off the far side.
Scattered. Flee to B1/C1 (entrance) or A3 (back exit) if weapons are drawn.

tacticalMap.gmTacticalNotes: Krev starts exposed at B1 — intentional. He's a collector, not a tactician. He expects compliance. The Kyuzo use Fleet Footed (2 maneuvers/turn) + Reposition (no reactions) to silently flank to A2 and D2 during the social phase — cutting off the booth row and alcove. This is positioning, not aggression. If combat triggers: Krev advances to B2 or C2 for melee range. Kyuzo attack with Hit and Run from flanking positions — headbutt then disengage. The bar (B3/C3) is the strongest defensive position. The open floor (C2) is the worst — no cover, clear sightlines. The back hallway (A3) is the escape route. The store room (D3) is a trap — full cover but no exit.

intel: Varga the Hutt sent a retrieval crew — this cylinder is worth something to him
The thugs were recovery, not assassination — Varga wants the item, not blood

pacing.openingBeat: Part 1 establishes Jakku, the crew's identity, the missing fixer, and the subdued cantina. Players get time to explore the room and its patrons. The pause gives them ownership of the space before the plot arrives.
pacing.risingAction: Part 2 — Maya crashes in wounded. Thugs fill the doorway. Krev delivers his ultimatum. Kyuzo silently flank. The crew must decide whether this stranger's problem is their problem.
pacing.climax: The decision — fast-draw intimidation, social play, or let it go to fists. One roll that matters.
pacing.resolution: The fight is done. The cantina is wrecked. The thugs are gone — one way or another. Maya is still here, bleeding at the table, watching you with an expression that is not quite gratitude.

disciplineChallenges.context: Reading Maya — is that collision really an accident? Only roll if a PC specifically says they're trying to read her. Failure matters: it hides that she angled toward their table on purpose, which colors trust going forward.
disciplineChallenges.narrativePacing: Roll only if asked. Do not prompt for this check.

control.failure: Maya reads as a desperate woman who tripped at the worst possible moment. Pure bad luck. No indication she chose their table — which she did.
control.mastery: The PC also catches something in the half-second before Maya 'tripped' — her eyes were already on their table. She tracked them before the fall. She's sharper than she's letting on, and this whole collision was a lot less accidental than it looked.

effect.fleeting: The wound is real. The fear is real. But there's something about the angle of her fall — she didn't stumble randomly. She was heading toward this table.
effect.masterful: She chose this table. She saw armed people and made a calculation mid-sprint. The trip was real — she's genuinely hurt — but the direction was tactical.
effect.legendary: All of the above, plus: her injuries are from a warning shot, not a real attempt to kill. Whoever shot her wanted to slow her down, not put her down. This is a retrieval job, not a hit. And that 'Don't move' wasn't panic — it was an instruction.

disciplineChallenges.context: The Gunslinger's fast draw IS this check — blaster out before the enforcers can react. The draw is the statement, the blaster is leverage. Other vocations can use presence, posture, or reputation. This is the ONE roll that determines whether violence happens.
disciplineChallenges.narrativePacing: This is a story fork. Always roll.

control.failure: Krev doesn't flinch. 'You won't shoot over some girl you don't know.' The PC must decide: pull the trigger (firefight) or holster it (Krev uses Lean In, smells weakness, pressure escalates).
control.mastery: The draw is so fast that one Kyuzo involuntarily steps back. Krev's crew is shaken — even if Krev holds, his backup has lost confidence.

effect.fleeting: Krev pauses. He's recalculating but hasn't decided to leave. 'Varga's going to hear about this.' Buys time but doesn't resolve — a second social action may be needed.
effect.masterful: 'Not worth it today.' Krev withdraws with the Kyuzo. Warns that Varga remembers faces. Clean resolution — no combat.
effect.legendary: Krev folds immediately and drops intel on the way out: 'Varga's got bigger problems than some cylinder — something about an Imperial deal gone wrong.' Seeds future plot threads.

disciplineChallenges.context: Talking the enforcers down or redirecting them. Appealing to Krev's professionalism — he's a retrieval man, not a killer. Failure matters: escalation.
disciplineChallenges.narrativePacing: Story fork. Always roll.

control.failure: Krev sees through it. 'Nice try. Hand her over.' Social approach has failed — next move is violence or surrender.

effect.fleeting: Krev hesitates — he's listening, but not convinced. He'll give one more chance to make a real offer. A second social action can close this.
effect.masterful: Thugs are mollified or confused enough to withdraw. Krev leaves muttering but doesn't escalate.
effect.legendary: Krev is genuinely charmed. Leaves on almost-friendly terms. 'You're smooth. Tell the girl Varga's patience isn't infinite, but today she gets a pass.'

disciplineChallenges.context: Claiming Maya already handed the cylinder off, or that there's a bigger play in motion. Selling a lie to a professional enforcer. Failure matters: escalation.
disciplineChallenges.narrativePacing: Story fork. Always roll.

control.failure: 'I know everyone who moves product for Varga. You're nobody.' The lie collapses. Krev's suspicion is now active hostility.

effect.fleeting: Krev hesitates — the story doesn't quite add up but he can't immediately disprove it. He'll leave to verify, buying the PCs time but not resolving the situation permanently.
effect.masterful: Krev buys it. Leaves to confirm with his boss. By the time he realizes the deception, the PCs are long gone.
effect.legendary: Krev completely buys it and offers an apology. 'Didn't know she had people. Tell your boss we're square.' The PCs have created a false paper trail that could be useful later.

scenes.title: Maya's Offer
scenes.subtitle: The Aftermath
scenes.readAloudPart1:
The cantina settles into a fragile quiet. Overturned chairs and broken glass crunch underfoot. The Bith bartender peers out from behind the bar, checking to see if it's safe. The few patrons who stayed press themselves into corners, deciding whether the show is over.

The woman at your table hasn't moved. She's sitting where she fell — one hand pressed against the blaster graze on her ribs, blood seeping between her fingers. But her eyes are clear. Sharp. She's been watching you the entire time.
scenes.readAloudPart1PauseNote: Let the players react. Someone may want to treat her wound (Medicine). Someone may want to read her (Insight). Someone may want to check outside (Survival). Give them a beat before she speaks.
scenes.readAloudPart2:
She straightens up. The desperation fades faster than it should — replaced by a cool, appraising focus that was always underneath.

"Your fixer isn't coming. Cade Ryll — that's who you're waiting for, right? I don't know what happened to him, but I know he's not walking through that door."

She reaches into her jacket, wincing, and produces a dull metallic cylinder about the length of a stylus.

"This is an Imperial Code Cylinder. Encrypted. I intercepted it before Varga's people could get their hands on it. I don't know exactly what's on it — but I know it's worth a fortune to the right buyer. The problem is, I can't decrypt it. There's a droid out in the badlands — Switch. He can crack anything."

She looks at each of you in turn.

"I need people who can handle themselves. You just proved you can. Come with me to Switch, help me figure out what this thing is, and I'll cut you in. Equal shares."
scenes.gmNotes:
BREATHER SCENE: This is the aftermath of the cantina confrontation. The energy should shift from adrenaline to calculation. Maya makes her pitch, the players decide whether to trust her, and someone can patch her wound. This is a character-building moment — let the players talk to each other, argue about whether to trust this stranger, and make the choice on their own terms.

MAYA'S PITCH: She's offering a partnership, not charity. She frames it as mutual need — she has the score, they have the muscle, and their fixer isn't coming. She mentions Cade Ryll by name to establish that she clocked them when she came in. She does NOT explain how she got the cylinder from Varga — she just says she intercepted it. If pressed, she deflects: 'Does it matter? It's here. They want it back. That's all you need to know.'

THE HOOK: The fixer no-show is the key. The PCs came to Jakku for a job. The job fell through. Maya is offering a replacement — and unlike the missing fixer, she's standing right here with Imperial hardware in her hand. The code cylinder is tangible, real, and obviously valuable enough that people are willing to fight over it.

IF THEY REFUSE: Maya leaves alone. Scene 2 still happens — the PCs hear about Switch through the cantina patrons (the Abyssin drifters know the badlands, Nela Bren knows Switch by reputation). But Maya goes to Switch without them, and the PCs lose their cut. This should feel like a missed opportunity, not a dead end.

npcs.name: Maya
npcs.notes: Solo smuggler. Wounded but calculating. She chose to crash into their table — the trip was real, the direction was deliberate.
npcs.behavior: Direct and transactional. No begging, no sob story. She reads the room — appeals to greed for mercenaries, justice for idealists, curiosity for investigators. Mentions the fixer by name to show she was paying attention. Deflects personal questions.
dialogue: "Your fixer isn't coming. Cade Ryll — that's who you're waiting for, right?"
"This is an Imperial Code Cylinder. Encrypted. I intercepted it before Varga's people could get their hands on it."
"There's a droid out in the badlands — Switch. He can crack anything. I need people who can handle themselves. You just proved you can."
"Come with me to Switch, help me figure out what this thing is, and I'll cut you in. Equal shares."
"Does it matter how I got it? It's here. They want it back. That's all you need to know."
npcs.intel: Maya intercepted an encrypted Imperial Code Cylinder from Varga's supply chain. She needs it decrypted by Switch, a droid information broker hidden in the badlands. She's offering equal shares of whatever the data leads to.

scenes.hazards: Post-brawl cantina. Broken furniture, nervous patrons. Varga's people could send a second crew.

decisions.choice: Accept Maya's offer — go to Switch together
decisions.consequence: The party heads into the badlands with Maya. Scene 3 (The Processor) follows directly.

decisions.choice: Demand more information before committing
decisions.consequence: Maya gives what she can but holds back personal details. Insight check reveals her motives. She won't wait forever.

decisions.choice: Refuse and let Maya go alone
decisions.consequence: Maya leaves. PCs can still find Switch through cantina patrons (Abyssin drifters, Nela Bren), but they lose their cut and Maya's trust.

decisions.choice: Try to take the cylinder from Maya
decisions.consequence: Maya is wounded but not stupid. She'll bolt. The cylinder leads nowhere without Switch — and the PCs have made an enemy of their best lead.

environmentMechanics.name: Wrecked Cantina
environmentMechanics.effect: Overturned furniture, broken glass, nervous patrons. The bartender Dodd Mull is cleaning up and wants everyone to leave. Tension lingers — a second crew from Varga could arrive.
environmentMechanics.mitigation: Keep voices low. Don't draw attention.

environmentMechanics.name: Ticking Clock
environmentMechanics.effect: Dodd Mull mentions that Krev will report back to Varga. The longer they stay at The Burning Deck, the more likely reinforcements arrive.
environmentMechanics.mitigation: Make a decision and move. The badlands are safer than the cantina right now.

intel: Maya intercepted an Imperial Code Cylinder from Varga the Hutt's supply chain
Cylinder needs a specialist — Switch, a droid hidden in the badlands
Their fixer Cade Ryll isn't coming — Maya is the only game in town
Maya is offering equal shares of whatever the data leads to
connections: Maya (potential partner — earned through the fight, not given freely)

pacing.openingBeat: Aftermath. The cantina is quiet. Maya is bleeding at the table. The players have a moment to breathe and process what just happened.
pacing.risingAction: Maya's pitch. She names the fixer, produces the cylinder, names Switch. The offer is transactional — she needs muscle, they need a job.
pacing.climax: The decision. Trust the stranger or walk away. The missing fixer makes this the only play on the table.
pacing.resolution: The party agrees (or doesn't). If yes, they head into the badlands. Clock is ticking — Varga will know.

disciplineChallenges.context: Treating Maya's blaster graze. Non-critical wound but painful. Treating her before she makes her pitch changes the dynamic — she's more forthcoming with someone who helped without being asked.
disciplineChallenges.narrativePacing: Skip the roll unless a player specifically wants to treat her. Reward the effort narratively. If no one offers, Maya patches herself with a wince and a strip torn from her jacket.

control.failure: Basic patch job. Bleeding stops but Maya winces through the rest of the conversation.

effect.fleeting: Wound cleaned and dressed competently. Maya is grateful — a brief, genuine moment before the mask goes back on.
effect.masterful: Professional treatment. Maya visibly relaxes and becomes more forthcoming with details about the code cylinder and her history with Varga.
effect.legendary: Expert field surgery — minimal scarring. Maya is impressed and trusts the PCs significantly more. She volunteers information she would otherwise hold back: the cylinder was moving through Varga's supply chain, and she thinks it connects to something Imperial.

disciplineChallenges.context: Reading Maya's motives. Is she telling the truth about the cylinder? Is she desperate or dangerous? What's she not saying?
disciplineChallenges.narrativePacing: Roll only if a player specifically asks to read her. This rewards suspicion without punishing trust.

control.failure: Maya is hard to read. She's clearly competent, clearly wounded, and clearly in a hurry. Beyond that — nothing.

effect.fleeting: She's genuinely afraid — not of the PCs, but of what happens if she goes to Switch alone. She needs backup.
effect.masterful: The crash into their table wasn't entirely accidental. She clocked them — armed crew, waiting for someone — and angled toward them on purpose. She's calculating, not desperate.
effect.legendary: Maya is holding back something about how she got the cylinder. She didn't just intercept it — she stole it deliberately. The fear isn't about Varga's thugs. It's about what's on the cylinder and who might come looking for it.

disciplineChallenges.context: Scouting the area outside The Burning Deck before heading into the badlands. Checking for a tail, reading the terrain, finding a safe route.
disciplineChallenges.narrativePacing: Optional. Rewards caution with tactical info. Good for players who want to feel prepared.

control.failure: The badlands are a maze of mesas and dry washes. Hard to see much from here.

effect.fleeting: No one followed the thugs out. The route to the Sinkhole is clear — for now.
effect.masterful: The PC identifies a sheltered approach through the dune field that avoids the main trails. Less chance of being spotted.
effect.legendary: The PC spots a second group — not Varga's people. Two figures watching The Burning Deck from a rooftop across the street. They're not enforcers. Imperial intelligence? Another interested party? They withdraw when spotted.

scenes.title: The Processor
scenes.gmNotes:
Switch is a crime lord and information broker, not an ally — he is a businessman. He examines the code cylinder and quotes a diagnostic fee the heroes can't afford. He then offers a deal: reduced fee in exchange for the heroes becoming information assets for his network. As they travel, they pass along intel — names, locations, movements, deals. If the intel is validated and leads to actionable resources, Switch compensates them. He also serves as a black market connection for gear, smuggling contacts, and fencing stolen goods.

The decryption reveals: the cylinder is an Imperial ghost transfer authorization device. It does NOT contain location data or vault coordinates. What it CAN do is authenticate into Imperial shuttle nav systems — essentially a master key for accessing flight computers on Imperial shuttles.

After the reveal, Maya connects the dots: she's been watching an Imperial Lambda-class shuttle sitting on the Reestkii Landing Field. If the cylinder can get them into the shuttle's nav computer, they can download its flight logs and destination data — which could lead to Varth and the fortune.

Switch should feel dangerous and calculating despite his droid body. His deal is transactional but honest.

npcs.name: Switch
npcs.notes: Crime lord and information broker. Decades without memory wipes. Thinks in long-term investments. His deal is transactional but honest.
npcs.behavior: Calculated precision. Uses silence as a weapon. Always running cost-benefit analysis.
dialogue: "Bring it here. Let me see what you've found."
"My standard diagnostic fee is... well, more than you have. But I see an opportunity for a longer arrangement."

"I reduce my fee. In exchange, you become assets in my intelligence network. You travel, you observe, you report. Validated intel earns compensation. Think of it as... mutually beneficial employment."

"Ghost transfer authorization. Military grade. This cylinder authenticates into Imperial shuttle nav computers — flight logs, destination data, everything in the system. Someone wanted access to Imperial movements."
npcs.intel: Switch identifies the code cylinder as ghost transfer authorization — a device that can authenticate into Imperial shuttle nav systems. It does not contain location data directly, but can access flight computers on Imperial vessels. He offers a deal: reduced diagnostic fee in exchange for the heroes becoming recurring information assets for his network.

threatBuild.notes: Switch is a social threat, not a physical one. His security systems are his teeth. Wits 4 with Informant +1 = effective 5, clamped to max. Resist 3 = Grit 3-1+1. Extremely hard to bluff or intimidate.

role.name: Informant
role.description: Intelligence operative. Trades secrets, exposes weaknesses, leverages information.

passive.name: Connected
passive.description: Always knows one piece of intel about each PC (GM chooses what).

actions.name: Expose Secret
actions.description: Social attack. Reveal damaging information. Targets Resist.

maneuver.name: Observe
maneuver.description: Study target. Learn one discipline rating or active condition.

gambit.name: Blackmail
gambit.description: If target doesn't comply, consequence is revealed to a third party.

exploit.name: Network
exploit.description: Summon reinforcements, deliver intel to a faction, or trigger a pre-arranged contingency.

classification.name: Standard
classification.description: Base template. Full stats, fixed action cadence. The workhorse NPC.
classification.notes: 2-4 Standards represent a meaningful encounter for a party.

threatBuild.name: Switch

scenes.hazards: Cramped underground bunker. Motion sensors active.

decisions.choice: Accept Switch's deal — reduced fee for becoming information assets
decisions.consequence: The cylinder is analyzed. Switch identifies it as ghost transfer authorization. Maya proposes using it on the shuttle at the landing field.

decisions.choice: Try to double-cross Switch
decisions.consequence: Switch has security turrets and backup. Extremely dangerous.

decisions.choice: Refuse the deal and try to decrypt elsewhere
decisions.consequence: No one else on Jakku has the capability. The cylinder stays locked.

narrativeLinks.note: Switch's agent TC-663 appears in Varga's court — connected through Switch's intelligence network.

encounters.name: The Deal with Switch
encounters.trigger: When heroes present the Code Cylinder
encounters.description: Switch examines the cylinder and quotes a diagnostic fee the heroes can't afford. He then offers an alternative: reduced fee in exchange for the heroes becoming information assets — passing along validated intel as they travel. This establishes Switch as a recurring contact and black market connection.
encounters.tactics: Patient and immovable. He knows what the cylinder is worth. Uses their financial desperation and Maya's impatience to steer toward his preferred deal.

encounters.name: The Analysis Reveal
encounters.trigger: After the deal is struck
encounters.description: The cylinder is an Imperial ghost transfer authorization — it can authenticate into Imperial shuttle nav systems. No location data, no vault coordinates. But it's a master key to flight computers. Maya connects the dots: the Lambda shuttle sitting on the Reestkii Landing Field could contain Varth's location in its nav logs.
encounters.tactics: Switch delivers with clinical detachment. Watches reactions carefully. He knows the cylinder's value and is already calculating angles.

environmentMechanics.name: Switch's Security Systems
environmentMechanics.trigger: Any aggressive action or theft attempt
environmentMechanics.effect: Hidden ceiling turrets deploy. Motion sensors track all movement. Single exit locks remotely.
environmentMechanics.mitigation: Don't threaten the droid. Treat this as a business meeting.

environmentMechanics.name: Cramped Quarters
environmentMechanics.effect: Narrow bunker cluttered with droid parts. No flanking. Melee range only.
environmentMechanics.mitigation: Use clutter for cover. Japor desk = indestructible hard cover.

intel: The code cylinder is ghost transfer authorization — authenticates into Imperial shuttle nav systems
No location data on the cylinder itself, but it can access Imperial flight computers
Maya has been tracking a Lambda shuttle at the Reestkii Landing Field
Cylinder references connect to 'Project Leviathan'
connections: Switch (info broker, recurring intelligence fixer, black market connection)

pacing.openingBeat: Descent into Switch's bunker beneath the Sinkhole. First impression: this droid is more than he seems.
pacing.risingAction: Negotiation. Switch quotes a fee they can't pay. Offers the intel-asset deal instead.
pacing.climax: The analysis reveal — the cylinder authenticates into Imperial shuttles. Maya proposes the heist.
pacing.resolution: Heroes know their target: the Lambda shuttle on the landing field. Clock is ticking.

disciplineChallenges.context: Negotiating Switch's terms for a better deal on the information asset arrangement. Switch is [Transactional] — his Resist drops by 2 when offered something he wants.
disciplineChallenges.narrativePacing: Roll if PCs push for better terms. Switch's default offer is fair but not generous.

control.failure: Standard terms stand — reduced fee, no advance. Switch is unmoved.

effect.fleeting: Minor concession — Switch throws in a data chip with local intel as a sweetener.
effect.masterful: Switch sweetens the deal — offers a small advance on future intel value.
effect.legendary: Switch offers partnership terms — ongoing intelligence sharing arrangement. The PCs have a droid broker in their pocket.

disciplineChallenges.context: Reading Switch's motives. What does this droid actually want?
disciplineChallenges.narrativePacing: Roll only if asked.

control.failure: Switch is inscrutable — droid body language gives nothing away.

effect.fleeting: Switch is genuinely interested in the cylinder — this is business, not a trap.
effect.masterful: Switch is building a network of obligations — positioning as the indispensable middleman across the Outer Rim.
effect.legendary: Switch is afraid. Something about the Imperial connection on this cylinder has him worried. He wants the PCs to succeed because it gets a dangerous asset out of his shop.

disciplineChallenges.context: Examining the cylinder data for hidden details after Switch's analysis.
disciplineChallenges.narrativePacing: Optional. Rewards curiosity with plot seeds.

control.failure: The analysis is straightforward — just authorization protocols.

effect.fleeting: The authorization codes are military-grade, not civilian. This cylinder came from someone important.
effect.masterful: Additional details: the authorization codes reference shipping routes connected to 'Project Leviathan.'
effect.legendary: The cylinder contains a ghost layer — hidden metadata showing it was deliberately copied, not stolen. Someone wanted this cylinder to leave the facility.

disciplineChallenges.context: Verifying Switch's analysis is genuine and complete — is the droid holding anything back?
disciplineChallenges.narrativePacing: Optional. Trust verification.

control.failure: No way to independently verify without comparable equipment.

effect.fleeting: Analysis appears complete based on surface-level review.
effect.masterful: Analysis is genuine and unaltered. Switch gave them everything.
effect.legendary: Analysis is complete, AND the PC notices Switch's equipment could decrypt the cylinder's secondary partition — but Switch didn't offer that service. There's more data locked inside.

scenes.readAloudPart1:
Maya leads you out of the settlement and into the open desert. The sun hammers down as you cross a dry wash and climb through a field of broken mesas — wind-carved rock pillars standing like the ribs of something ancient. No tracks. No markers. Nothing out here but heat and silence and the whisper of sand moving against itself.

Maya moves with purpose. She knows this path.
scenes.readAloudPart1PauseNote: Let the players take in the desert. If anyone wants to scout or investigate the surroundings, this is the moment. Ask: "What do you notice as you walk?" or "Are you watching for anything specific?" Then move to Part 2 when they reach the bunker.
scenes.readAloudPart2:
The sinkhole opens without warning — a ragged crater in the mesa floor, maybe thirty meters across, its walls carved smooth by millennia of wind. At the bottom, half-buried in sand, sits what looks like an abandoned moisture vaporator station. Maya slides down the slope and keys in a code on a rusted panel. A blast door grinds open.

Inside is a technological cave — wires hang from the ceiling like vines, the floor is a carpet of droid parts and discarded components. Screens flicker with data feeds from across the sector. But in the center of the filth sits a pristine, priceless desk of Japor ivory wood — polished to a mirror shine, completely out of place in this desert hole.

Behind it, a protocol droid who has clearly forgone memory wipes for decades regards you with an unsettling intelligence. His photoreceptors track each of you individually as you enter.

"Ah. Maya. You brought friends." The droid's vocabulator carries the precise, faintly condescending tone of old Republic diplomatic programming. "How unlike you."
scenes.subtitle: The Sinkhole

scenes.title: The Gangster's Revenge
scenes.readAloud:
The bunker's motion sensors scream. Before you can react, the hatch above explodes inward. Standing in the smoking frame is a massive Chevin — Ganga Lor, Varga the Hutt's lieutenant. His trunk curls with fury as he levels a heavy blaster at the room.

"Nobody steals from the Mighty Varga," he growls, his voice like grinding stone. "Not the girl. Not the droid. And definitely not you."
scenes.gmNotes:
Ganga Lor wants to recover the code cylinder and make an example of everyone involved. Varga wants the cylinder because it could lead someone to Varth before Varga gets to him — Varga needs Varth alive to unlock the encrypted account in his own financial network.

The fight takes place in cramped quarters — use the environment: toppling droid part stacks, shooting steam pipes, activating Switch's security turrets. This is a significant combat — Ganga Lor is a real threat.

GANGA LOR IS KILLED IN THIS FIGHT. This is a mandatory outcome. He does not escape or retreat. His death establishes that the heroes' actions have real consequences and removes Varga's local enforcer from the board.

After the fight, if the deal with Switch was struck earlier, Switch finishes the cylinder analysis and delivers the ghost transfer authorization reveal. If the analysis was already complete, the fight simply confirms that Varga is actively hunting the cylinder.

scenes.title: The Heist
scenes.readAloud:
The Reestkii Landing Field stretches out in the heat haze — a militarized zone on the outskirts of the settlement. Through your macrobinoculars, you see it: a Lambda-class Imperial shuttle sitting on the landing pad, flanked by stormtroopers in transition-era armor, dirty from the desert but standing sharp.

The shuttle's nav computer holds the answers — flight logs, destination data, coordinates. But the cylinder only works at the terminal. You have to get inside the shuttle, plug in, authenticate, and download before anyone notices you don't belong here.

The clock is ticking. Imperial pickup crews rotate on a schedule, and the next shift change is less than an hour out.
scenes.gmNotes:
This is the heroes' first brush with the Young Empire. The target is the Lambda shuttle's nav computer. The heroes must physically reach the shuttle, use the code cylinder to authenticate into the flight computer, and download the destination coordinates — which reveal the Vanishing Place on Ajan Kloss, where Admiral Varth is being held.

OPTIONS:
• BLUFF: Impersonate Imperial crew or authorized maintenance personnel. Disguise Kit helps. Deception check (Moderate) to approach without suspicion.
• STEALTH: Sneak through maintenance areas or use terrain cover to reach the shuttle unseen. Stealth/Skulduggery (Moderate).
• DISTRACTION: Create a diversion to draw the guards away. Tech, Deception, or creative approaches (Moderate).
• DIRECT ASSAULT: Possible but loud. Brings reinforcements quickly. Not recommended but not forbidden.

THE DOWNLOAD: Once at the shuttle's flight computer, a Tech check (Easy with the cylinder, Moderate without) accesses the nav logs. The coordinates point to the jungle moon of Ajan Kloss — a location labeled 'The Vanishing Place' in Imperial records. A successful Investigation check on the data reveals references to 'Project Leviathan' in the shipping metadata.

EXTRACTION: Once the download is complete (or the alarm is raised), the heroes must escape the landing field. Maya has the Banshee prepped at a secondary pad. The escape from Jakku transitions directly into Part 2.

IMPORTANT: The code cylinder is effectively dead after use. The authentication codes will be flagged and disabled by Imperial security within hours.

scenes.hazards: Militarized hangar — heavy machinery, incoming Imperial shuttle providing time pressure. Open terrain with limited cover.

decisions.choice: Bluff approach — impersonate Imperial personnel
decisions.consequence: Requires Deception checks. Lower risk if successful. Disguise Kit helps.

decisions.choice: Stealth approach — sneak to the shuttle
decisions.consequence: Requires Stealth/Skulduggery. Avoids confrontation until extraction.

decisions.choice: Create a diversion
decisions.consequence: Draws guards away but risks escalating the situation.

decisions.choice: Direct assault
decisions.consequence: Loud and fast. Guarantees combat. Reinforcements arrive quickly.

narrativeLinks.note: The nav data downloaded here provides the Ajan Kloss coordinates that set up the Part 2 extraction.

encounters.name: The Approach
encounters.trigger: Heroes begin their approach to the shuttle
encounters.description: Multiple approach vectors to the Lambda shuttle. Stormtroopers patrol the perimeter. Warlord's guards control the outer fence. The heroes need to reach the shuttle's cockpit to access the nav computer.
encounters.tactics: Stormtroopers are disciplined but bored. Warlord's guards are sloppy. Exploit the jurisdictional friction between Imperial and local forces.

encounters.name: The Download
encounters.trigger: Heroes reach the shuttle nav computer
encounters.description: Use the code cylinder to authenticate into the flight computer. Download the nav logs. The coordinates reveal the Vanishing Place on Ajan Kloss. Time-limited — the download takes several rounds, during which discovery is possible.
encounters.tactics: Tech check with the cylinder is Easy. Without it, Moderate and takes longer. Investigation check reveals Project Leviathan references in the metadata.

encounters.name: The Escape
encounters.trigger: Alarm raised or download complete
encounters.description: Heroes must escape the landing field and reach Maya at the Banshee. Stormtroopers pursue. The escape from Jakku transitions into Part 2.
encounters.tactics: Running firefight across the landing field. Maya has the Banshee prepped at a secondary pad. Once aboard, they break atmosphere and jump.

environmentMechanics.mitigation: Bluff, stealth, or neutralize guards before they trigger the alarm.

environmentMechanics.mitigation: Coolth Backpack or quick infiltration.

environmentMechanics.mitigation: Use cargo and vehicles as mobile cover. Fuel drums are explosive.

Project Leviathan referenced in shuttle metadata
The code cylinder is dead after use — authentication codes will be disabled

pacing.resolution: Data acquired. Escape to the Banshee. Break atmosphere. Jump to hyperspace.

disciplineChallenges.narrativePacing: Always roll. This is the mission objective.

scenes.title: The Banshee
scenes.readAloud:
Maya banks the Banshee hard as you clear Jakku's atmosphere, the desert world shrinking behind you. She's already plotting the jump — not to a standard hyperspace lane, but to what she calls the Smuggler's Path, a stitched-together chain of scavenger trails, shadow-port routes, and poorly maintained back-lanes that only the desperate or the skilled attempt.

"The Empire patrols the main lanes out of Arkanis Sector," she says, not looking up from the nav computer. "Hydian Way, Corellian Run — every checkpoint from here to the Core is Imperial. We go the long way. Four jumps, four dropouts. Each one, we recalculate."

She taps the nav display — a jagged line appears, zig-zagging northeast through the Mid Rim and Expansion Region, threading the gap between patrol corridors. "First jump takes us to a dead spot near the Trax Tube — no stations, no patrols, nothing but empty space. We recalculate there, pick up a shadow-port route through the Expansion Region, then hop to Ord Mantell for fuel and nav data. After that, one more jump to Aldhani — Imperial garrison world, so we stay in orbit just long enough to lock coordinates — then straight into the Cademimu sector."

Then she glances at the co-pilot seat — empty — and back at one of you. A half-smile. "You fly, right? Get up here. I need someone on the secondaries who knows what a yaw compensator does."

The Smuggler's Path is a nightmare of constant recalculation, blind jumps into empty space, and the ever-present risk of dropping out too close to an Imperial patrol. Maya flies with a confidence that borders on recklessness — but she's teaching as she goes, calling out readings, sharing the stick on the tight corrections. Reading the gaps between hyperspace lanes like she's done it before — and for the first time, letting someone else read them with her.

Four jumps. Four dropouts. Each one tenser than the last — the deep space recalculation where comms are dead and you're utterly alone, the white-knuckle slingshot past a gravity well near Ord Mantell, the heart-stopping moment an Imperial sensor buoy pings the Banshee near Aldhani before Maya kills the transponder and dives back into hyperspace.

When you finally drop out for the last time, the viewport fills with green. Ajan Kloss — a jungle moon orbiting a gas giant in the Cademimu Sector. And in orbit, barely visible against the planet's curve, the angular silhouette of a Victory-class Star Destroyer.
scenes.gmNotes:
Maya owns and flies the Banshee. She is the pilot for the entire campaign. This scene establishes her competence, her ship, and the co-pilot dynamic with the Ace.

CO-PILOT SEAT: Maya invites whoever has piloting skill (ideally the party's Ace Pilot) to sit co-pilot. This is NOT a test — it's a genuine invitation born from practicality and the beginnings of trust. She teaches them to read the Path, calls out corrections, shares the stick on tight maneuvers. This is the Ace Pilot's first spotlight moment. If no one has piloting skill, Maya handles it solo but comments that she could use a co-pilot — planting the seed.

BONDING MOMENT: The Smuggler's Path run is a crucible. Maya and the co-pilot are working in sync under pressure — and Maya lets her guard down. She stops performing the 'cool smuggler' act and becomes someone focused, skilled, and genuinely collaborative. This is the first real human connection she makes with the crew.

THE SMUGGLER'S PATH: A stitched-together chain of scavenger trails, shadow-port routes, and poorly maintained back-lanes that avoids Imperial patrols. Maya has run variations of it before — she's a smuggler, and routes like this are how the Outer Rim moves contraband. The route requires four separate hyperspace jumps with manual recalculation at each dropout point:

JUMP 1: Jakku (I-13) → Deep Space Dropout (~J-12). Exit scavenger trails into empty space near the Trax Tube corridor. No stations, no patrols — just dead space. Recalculate to pick up shadow-port routes heading coreward.

JUMP 2: Deep Space → Expansion Region Gap (~K-9). Threading between patrol corridors through the Mid Rim. Drop out in the gap between the Lesser Lantillian Route and the Corellian Trade Spine. Recalculate for the run to Ord Mantell.

JUMP 3: Expansion Region → Ord Mantell (L-7). Refuel at the junkyard bazaars. Buy fresh nav data for the Cademimu sector from black-market astrogators. The crew has a few hours here — enough to resupply or get into trouble.

JUMP 4: Ord Mantell → Aldhani (M-6). Imperial garrison world in the Cademimu sector. Maya drops out just long enough to lock final coordinates for the Ajara system, then punches it before Imperial sensors can get a clean read.

FINAL JUMP: Aldhani → Ajan Kloss (L-5). Short jump into the Ajara system. Arrive at the jungle moon.

The Ace co-pilot makes real contributions at each dropout: calling out gravity shadows, adjusting deflector angles, managing power distribution during recalculation windows.

AJAN KLOSS APPROACH: Upon arrival, the heroes detect a Victory-class Star Destroyer in orbit and V-wing patrols. The Vanishing Place is on the surface. Landing must avoid Imperial detection — Maya identifies a canyon approach that masks their signature from orbital sensors.

NARRATIVE FUNCTION: This scene transitions from Part 1 to Part 2. It establishes:
1. Maya as the crew's pilot and ship owner
2. The co-pilot dynamic — the Ace is not a passenger, they're crew
3. The Banshee as the party's transport
4. The Imperial military presence at Ajan Kloss
5. The urgency — Varth is being held at the Vanishing Place and the Empire is about to take custody

This is the first time she treats someone like crew instead of a passenger. After arrival, shifts to tactical — assessing the orbital situation and planning the landing approach.
dialogue: "The Empire patrols the main lanes out of Arkanis Sector. We go through the Maze or we don't go at all."
"You fly, right? Get up here. I need someone on the secondaries who knows what a yaw compensator does."
"See that gravity shadow? Call it out before I hit it next time. That's what co-pilots do."

"I can get us down through the canyon approach. Their sensors won't pick us up in the magnetic interference. Probably."
npcs.intel: Maya has run routes like the Smuggler's Path before — stitching together scavenger trails and shadow-port lanes to avoid Imperial checkpoints. She knows the Banshee's limits and pushes them. She has no idea what the Vanishing Place is, but the Imperial presence tells her it matters. The co-pilot invitation is genuine — she needs the help and she's starting to trust the crew.

scenes.title: The Crash
scenes.readAloud:
You break the cloud layer and the lush, overgrown jungle of Ajan Kloss rushes up to meet you. The moon is a Green Hell — massive ancient trees, fungal overgrowth, and air so thick with humidity your viewport fogs instantly.

Then the proximity alarm screams. Two V-wings, dropping out of a patrol pattern — they've spotted you. Laser fire stitches across the Banshee's hull. The cockpit fills with sparks and the shriek of shearing metal.

Maya jerks sideways in the pilot's seat. A jagged piece of shrapnel from the shattered viewport frame has punched through her left shoulder. Blood spreads fast across her flight jacket. Her hands slip on the stick.

"I can't—" She grits her teeth, pulls her hands back from the controls. "Take it. Take the stick. NOW."

The jungle rushes up. The Banshee is shuddering, starboard engine trailing smoke, and the pilot is bleeding out in the seat next to you. The canyon walls are closing in. You have maybe ten seconds to put this ship on the ground in one piece.

The landing is controlled chaos — you bring the Banshee down hard in a narrow clearing, shearing branches and gouging earth. The ship groans to a stop, listing to port.

Maya is pale, pressing her hand against the wound. "Good landing," she manages. "Now get me a medpac before I pass out."
scenes.gmNotes:
V-WING STRAFING: Two V-wings on patrol detect the Banshee during the canyon approach and open fire. The cockpit takes damage — shrapnel from the shattered viewport frame hits Maya in the left shoulder. This is a serious wound: she's bleeding, losing grip strength, and cannot fly. The Ace Pilot (or whoever is in the co-pilot seat) must take the stick and bring the Banshee down.

ACE TAKES THE STICK: This is THE Ace Pilot spotlight moment. Maya is wounded and physically cannot fly. The Ace must perform a controlled crash landing — Piloting check (Hard). Success means a clean enough landing; failure means additional hull damage and injuries. Either way, they get down alive. The GM should make this feel earned — the jungle canopy, the canyon walls, the failing instruments, the smoke.

MAYA'S WOUND: Real and serious. Shrapnel to the left shoulder — not life-threatening with proper treatment, but she's lost blood and can't use her left arm fully. A Medicine check stabilizes her. She's conscious and lucid — and immediately starts thinking about repairs.

MAYA STAYS BEHIND: Maya stays with the Banshee to repair. She gives the heroes TWO objectives:
1. PRIMARY: Find and extract Varth.
2. SECONDARY: Disable the perimeter turret defenses. Maya has detected automated turret emplacements ringing the fortress from the Banshee's damaged sensors. If those turrets are active when she tries to fly extraction, she'll be shot down. "I can't come get you if those guns are still up. Find a way to kill them or I'm flying into a kill box."

This creates a hard permission gate: turrets up = dangerous extraction. Turrets down = clean extraction. This ties directly to the generator sabotage in Mandrake's deal.

IMPORTANT: Maya does NOT accompany the heroes into the jungle or the fortress. She stays with the Banshee. This is both practical (someone needs to fix the ship and she's wounded) and narrative (her conditional extraction is the climax payoff).

"The starboard engine took the worst of it. I can fix it, but I need time. And tools. And for someone to stop the bleeding."

"Listen — I picked up turret emplacements on the approach. Automated defense grid ringing the fortress. If those are still active when I come to get you, I'm flying into a kill box. You need to find a way to shut them down."
"Go. Find your admiral. Kill those turrets. I'll have the Banshee ready."

scenes.title: The Jungle Trek
scenes.readAloud:
The jungle is alive — and hungry. Every step forward is a negotiation with the terrain. Acid pools disguised as standing water hiss when a thrown rock disturbs them. Fungal blooms the size of speeder bikes pulse with a sickly bioluminescence, and you quickly learn that disturbing them triggers a violent detonation of toxic spores.

Then the ambush comes — not from Imperials, but from a ghost. A lone figure drops from the canopy — lean, hard, wearing tattered Separatist commando gear patched with strips of woven plant fiber and beadwork you don't recognize. Indigenous craftsmanship. Whoever this man is, he's been here a long time.

His rifle is aimed at your heads. But his finger isn't on the trigger. He's studying you.
scenes.gmNotes:
Environmental hazards: Acid Pools (disguised as standing water) and Exploding Fungus Blooms (detonate when disturbed). The ambusher is Vazus Mandrake, a Separatist Holdout Commando who refused the shutdown order 3 years ago. He's gone native — adopted the indigenous culture of Ajan Kloss, learned their language, wears their craftsmanship woven into his old gear.

MANDRAKE'S MOTIVATION (REVISED): Mandrake is NOT fighting a generic guerrilla war against Skarn. He's protecting the indigenous people of Ajan Kloss who took him in after his cause died. When Skarn's warlord forces occupied the Vanishing Place, they displaced local indigenous communities and rounded up those who resisted. These prisoners are held inside the fortress behind laser fences and security doors — all powered by the central generator.

Mandrake's deal is personal: guide the heroes inside through a secret drainage sluice in exchange for sabotaging the generator. The generator powers FOUR critical systems: (1) perimeter turret defenses, (2) laser fences penning the prisoners, (3) security doors blocking the detention area, and (4) the sensor grid. Destroying it accomplishes everything: honors his deal (frees the prisoners), clears Maya's extraction flight path (turrets down), opens access to the detention block (security doors down), and creates chaos cover.

THE FOUR-PURPOSE CONVERGENCE: When the heroes learn about the generator from Mandrake, they should realize that sabotaging it also solves Maya's turret problem from Scene 2. One action, four payoffs. This is the moment the plan clicks together.

Once convinced the heroes are against the Empire (or at least against Skarn), Mandrake reveals the full picture. He's been planning this for years but lacked the firepower to do it alone.

npcs.name: Vazus Mandrake
npcs.notes: Separatist commando gone native. Adopted indigenous culture of Ajan Kloss. Protects local population from Skarn's occupation. Wears CIS gear patched with indigenous beadwork and woven plant fiber.
npcs.behavior: Paranoid and precise. Tests the heroes before revealing himself. Ambushes from the canopy — drops down with rifle aimed but finger off the trigger. Once convinced they're against Skarn, reveals his real motivation: freeing indigenous prisoners held inside the fortress. Becomes a cautious but genuine ally. Speaks with deep conviction about 'his people' — and he means the locals, not the Separatists.
dialogue: "Don't move. I've had a bead on you since the river crossing."
"You're going to The Vanishing Place? Then we have a common interest. Skarn has my people penned up inside that fortress."
"When his forces moved in, the locals resisted. Skarn rounded up everyone who fought back. They're behind laser fences and locked doors — all powered by one generator."

"I know a way in — a drainage sluice on the northeast face. But I need something in return. The generator. I need it destroyed. It powers the turret defenses, the fences, the security doors, and the sensor grid. One target, and everything Skarn built falls apart."
"They took me in when my war ended. I won't leave them in cages."
npcs.intel: Mandrake knows the fortress layout intimately. He's been running recon for years. He knows guard rotations, blind spots, the drainage sluice entrance, and critically, the generator's role in powering turrets, laser fences, security doors, and the sensor grid. He also knows Vischera is conducting experiments in the Medical Bay. His indigenous people — the locals who adopted him — are held in detention pens near the fortress's east wing.

He offers to guide the heroes inside via a drainage sluice in exchange for sabotaging the generator. The generator powers four systems: turret defenses, laser fences, security doors, and the sensor grid. This is the convergence point — one sabotage action solves Maya's turret problem, frees the prisoners, opens the detention block, and blinds the sensors.

environmentMechanics.name: Acid Pools

environmentMechanics.mitigation: Tox detector identifies automatically. Thrown rock test. Survival check to spot visual tells.

environmentMechanics.name: Exploding Fungus Blooms
environmentMechanics.trigger: Physical contact with the fungal formations
environmentMechanics.effect: Violent spore detonation in 5m radius. Toxic spores cause Endure trauma and temporary respiratory distress.
environmentMechanics.mitigation: Breath mask blocks spore damage. Ranged detonation from safe distance. Stealth to navigate around.

environmentMechanics.name: Toxic Atmosphere (Canopy Level)
environmentMechanics.effect: Fungal spore density increases near the canopy. Extended exposure without protection causes escalating fatigue.
environmentMechanics.mitigation: Breath mask. Stay low. Move through open clearings when possible.

environmentMechanics.name: Hostile Fauna
environmentMechanics.effect: Ajan Kloss wildlife is territorial and predatory. Large arthropods, hunting birds, venomous ground-crawlers.
environmentMechanics.mitigation: Stealth to avoid. Beast Handling to redirect. Combat as last resort — gunfire attracts attention.

intel: Detailed fortress layout from Mandrake
Guard rotation patterns
Location of the drainage sluice entrance
Captain Vischera's experiments in the Medical Bay
Generator powers four systems: turrets, laser fences, security doors, sensor grid
Indigenous prisoners held in east wing detention pens

connections: Vazus Mandrake (guide, ally — adopted indigenous culture, fights for his people)

pacing.openingBeat: The jungle closes in. Every step is a negotiation with the terrain.
pacing.risingAction: Environmental hazards escalate. Acid pools, fungus, hostile fauna.
pacing.climax: Mandrake's ambush. Rifle in your face from the canopy. But his finger isn't on the trigger.
pacing.resolution: The deal is struck — and the plan clicks. Generator sabotage solves everything: frees prisoners, kills turrets, opens doors, blinds sensors. Mandrake leads them toward the fortress.

disciplineChallenges.context: Identifying acid pools before someone steps in one. The pools look like normal jungle puddles.
disciplineChallenges.narrativePacing: Roll when the party approaches standing water.

control.failure: Someone steps in. Acid damage to boots/legs. The pool is shallow but corrosive.

effect.fleeting: Subtle discoloration spotted — "Don't step there." One pool identified.
effect.masterful: Pattern recognized — dead vegetation around the edges gives them away. All pools in the area identified.
effect.legendary: The PC identifies the acid source — a mineral seep from the facility's waste system. The seep trail leads directly toward the fortress drainage outflow.

disciplineChallenges.context: Navigating difficult jungle terrain — root systems, steep ravines, mudslides.
disciplineChallenges.narrativePacing: Roll at major terrain obstacles.

control.failure: Someone slips or falls. Minor injury, time lost, noise made.

effect.fleeting: Slow but safe progress. Extra time spent finding handholds.
effect.masterful: Good time through the obstacle. No injuries from the terrain.
effect.legendary: The PC finds the fastest route and helps the whole party through efficiently. Time saved.

disciplineChallenges.context: Moving through the jungle without alerting Imperial patrols or territorial predators.
disciplineChallenges.narrativePacing: Roll when approaching known patrol areas.

control.failure: Something notices them — a territorial predator, a sensor drone, or a patrol. Encounter triggered.

effect.fleeting: Slip past but leave traces. The next stealth check will be harder — they're looking.
effect.masterful: Silent progress. No encounters with hostile fauna or Imperial patrols.
effect.legendary: Ghost through the jungle. Also observe a patrol pattern that reveals a gap in fortress security.

disciplineChallenges.context: Reading Mandrake during his ambush — is he going to shoot? What does he want?
disciplineChallenges.narrativePacing: Roll when Mandrake appears. Critical social read.

control.failure: Impossible to read. The tattered uniform and hard eyes give nothing away. He could be anything.
control.mastery: The PC also notices Mandrake's weapon is Imperial issue but modified — he's ex-military, not a deserter. He LEFT the service.

effect.fleeting: He's not going to shoot immediately. His finger isn't on the trigger.
effect.masterful: He's testing you. He wants something — this is a negotiation, not an ambush.
effect.legendary: He's desperate. The tattered uniform is from months in the jungle. He needs the generator gone because it powers the perimeter that keeps him trapped in this sector.

disciplineChallenges.context: Negotiating with Mandrake for guidance to the fortress without committing to the generator sabotage.
disciplineChallenges.narrativePacing: Roll if PCs try to negotiate different terms. Mandrake's default: sabotage or nothing.

control.failure: No deal without the sabotage. Mandrake is immovable on this point.

effect.fleeting: Mandrake reduces his demand — sabotage the generator OR provide him with supplies and a comm unit.
effect.masterful: Mandrake agrees to guide them to the entrance. The sabotage becomes optional — he'll accept a promise of extraction instead.
effect.legendary: Full alliance. Mandrake guides them in, provides detailed facility intel, and agrees to create a diversion when they're ready to extract.

scenes.subtitle: Ajan Kloss

scenes.title: The Vanishing Place — Infiltration
scenes.readAloud:
From the ridge, you see the fortress. It is not a pristine Imperial garrison but a repurposed, moss-covered temple ruin fortified with scavenged Clone Wars technology. Laser fences line the perimeter. Gun emplacements squat on the walls like armored toads.

And there, on the landing pad — a Lambda-class shuttle. A squad of pristine Stormtroopers stands at attention, a stark contrast to Skarn's grimy mercenaries. The Empire is already here.
scenes.gmNotes:
Mandrake leads the heroes through the drainage sluice into the fortress interior. Once inside, Mandrake splits off — he positions himself near the east wing detention pens where his people are held, ready to move the moment the generator goes down. He tells the heroes: 'I'll be at the pens. When the lights go out, I'm getting them out. You get your admiral. We meet at the north clearing for extraction.'

Key locations: the Medical Bay (Captain Vischera conducts biological experiments on wildlife), the Communications Center, and the Generator Room. Intercepted transmissions reveal that the Imperial officer outside is stalling — Inquisitor Valin Draco is en route to take personal custody of Varth. This creates a hard clock: grab Varth before the Inquisitor lands.

GENERATOR SABOTAGE (UPDATED): The generator encounter now has four-purpose significance. When sabotaged, it simultaneously: (1) kills the perimeter turret defenses — clearing Maya's extraction flight path, (2) drops the laser fences penning the indigenous prisoners — enabling Mandrake's rescue, (3) unlocks the security doors blocking the detention area, and (4) blinds the sensor grid — creating chaos cover. The GM should make this payoff chain explicit when the generator blows.

npcs.name: Captain Vischera
npcs.notes: On loan to Skarn. Conducting biological experiments in the Medical Bay.
npcs.behavior: Cold, clinical, and fascinated by the jungle's biology. Not a fighter. Will surrender if cornered. Tries to negotiate with scientific data as leverage.
dialogue: "I'm a researcher, not a soldier. The specimens here are worth more than whatever you're after."
"You want to know what Skarn is really doing here? I can tell you. Just don't touch my samples."
"The Imperial officer in the holding cells? Charming man. Completely amoral."
npcs.intel: Vischera knows Draco is coming specifically for Varth. She knows Skarn is stalling the Imperial handoff to extract more payment. She knows Varth has a memorized authentication key that he considers essential — something tied to financial records — but she doesn't know the details.

npcEffects.legendary: Chassis damage + [Rattled] + target must move 1 zone (Controller's choice)

npcs.notes: Grimy, undisciplined compared to Stormtroopers. Mixed species.
npcs.behavior: Patrol in pairs. Bored and complacent in the fortress interior. Alert near the detention block and communications center. Respond to alarms slowly.
dialogue: "Did you hear something in the sluice?"
"Skarn says no one goes near the Imperial's cell without authorization."
npcs.intel: The mercs know Skarn is playing a dangerous game with the Empire. Morale is low — they're afraid of Draco.

maneuver.name: Lockdown
maneuver.description: Modifies Move. The Anchor does not move. Instead, enemies in the Anchor's zone cannot move to another zone without succeeding on a Physique check vs Anchor's Defense.

gambit.name: Shove Back
gambit.description: In addition to normal effects, push target 1 zone. The Anchor holds position.

exploit.name: Intercept
exploit.trigger: An enemy in the Anchor's zone attacks an ally.
exploit.description: The Anchor becomes the target of the attack instead.

threatBuild.notes: Minion guards. Defense 1 = max(2,2)-1+0. Grimy and undisciplined — easier to intimidate than Stormtroopers. Masterful+ = takedown.

npcs.name: Stormtrooper Squad
npcs.notes: Pristine armor. At the landing pad near the shuttle. Separate from Skarn's people.
npcs.behavior: Maintain their own perimeter. Don't mix with mercs. Professional and alert. Answer only to the Imperial officer on site.
dialogue: "This area is under Imperial jurisdiction. Move along."
npcs.intel: The troopers know Draco is en route. They're the advance team. Their orders are to secure the landing pad and wait.

scenes.hazards: Fortified temple ruin — laser fences, gun emplacements, Clone Wars-era tech security. Hard clock: Inquisitor Draco is en route.

decisions.choice: Explore the Medical Bay before pushing deeper

encounters.tactics: Slow, careful progress. Skulduggery to disarm traps. Tech to bypass sensors. Athletics for the tight squeeze.

encounters.name: The Medical Bay
encounters.trigger: Optional — the heroes can explore the medical bay

encounters.tactics: Vischera is not hostile. She can be interrogated, intimidated, or bargained with. She values her research above her loyalty to Skarn.

encounters.name: The Communications Center
encounters.trigger: Optional — the heroes can access the comms center

environmentMechanics.mitigation: Generator sabotage disables all laser fences. Tech check can disable individual sections.
